title: A lost world of perfect diction
gist: A short notebook trio: a lament that the impeccable spoken diction of postwar literary Britain has vanished, a recommendation of Julie Roset's recording of 20th-century French chansons, and Paul Preston's biography crediting Juan Carlos's duplicity with enabling Spain's democratic transition.

insights.claim: A 1970 documentary on Virginia Woolf shows talking heads like Elizabeth Bowen speaking with a clarity and eloquence of diction that the author argues has almost entirely disappeared.
insights.so_what: A note on declining standards of public spoken English as a cultural marker.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay

insights.claim: Soprano Julie Roset's 'M'a dit Amour', accompanied by Susan Manoff, curates 20th-century French chansons and foregrounds female composers, especially the short-lived Lili Boulanger.
insights.so_what: Signals renewed attention to neglected female composers of the French chanson repertoire.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay

insights.claim: Paul Preston's 'Downfall of a King' credits Juan Carlos with a leading role in Spain's transition to democracy after Franco's 1975 death, arguing his very duplicity let him outwit the dying dictator.
insights.so_what: A counterintuitive read: personal vice as the enabling trait of a democratic transition.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
